speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Auto-Merge - Merging When You Can't Wait Around into plain language. Auto-merge lets you pre-authorize a PR to merge automatically the moment all branch protection requirements are satisfied - required reviews approved, all status checks passing, and the branch up to date. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. You've addressed all review comments and are waiting for CI to finish. A maintainer has approved the PR but a required check is still running. You're in a different time zone from the reviewers.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The PR merge box changes to show "Auto-merge enabled - merge will happen automatically". When the last required check passes (or last required review arrives), GitHub merges the PR silently. You receive a notification: "Your PR was automatically merged". If a reviewer requests changes after auto-merge is enabled, auto-merge is automatically cancelled (a new approval is required before it re-enables).

speaker: ALEX

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What Reviewers Look For. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on What Reviewers Look For. When a reviewer opens your PR, they are asking four questions before they ever look at the diff. This is the part to say slowly: If your description answers all four, the reviewer can jump straight into the code with context.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Why does this change exist? -- What problem does it solve, or what goal does it advance?; What is the scope? -- Which files changed, and roughly how big is the change?; How was it tested? -- Did you verify that the change works, and how?; and What should I pay attention to? -- Are there tricky parts, trade-offs, or areas where you want a second opinion? Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub recognizes special keywords in PR descriptions that automatically close linked issues when the PR merges. The listener should be able to check this: You do not need to close issues by hand -- just include the right keyword followed by the issue number.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat Before/After Structure as decoration. This gives the reviewer an instant mental model of what changed without reading the diff line by line. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Use Markdown headings ( ) in your description (Summary, Changes, Related Issues, Testing) so reviewers can press H to jump between sections. Type Closes followed by the issue number to auto-close the linked issue on merge; GitHub autocompletes when you type. Press Ctrl+Shift+P in the description text area to toggle between Write and Preview modes; Preview renders your Markdown so you can check structure before submitting. Use the Preview tab to verify your Markdown renders correctly; headings, bullet lists, and code blocks are easier to proofread in rendered form. Keep bullet points short (one line each) so the description is scannable at high zoom without excessive horizontal scrolling. When including screenshots, add alt text in the Markdown image syntax: so every reader gets the same information.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, navigate to Pull Requests (G then P in Focus Mode). Then, open the first PR in the list (press Enter on its title). How did the author respond? The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: You should be able to point to the evidence, explain the action, and describe what you would do next if this were a real open source project. If you can teach the move back, you have learned it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if they get stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: Read the latest message, not the loudest worry. Check the issue, the branch, the pull request, the status check, or the bot comment. Then ask for help with those facts in hand. That is how professionals collaborate.
